narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 59.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 24, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1st decile, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27 and undergraduate GPA is 3.24, while successful full-time students make up 93% of passers—your profile aligns with full-time attendance but stands out in other areas. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 decile ranking, which significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar exam failure, as it reflects performance well below what we've seen in those who succeed. Next in influence was your family income bracket, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, since lower-middle incomes are less common but not barriers among bar passers (only 9.4% of them). Your LSAT score followed as the third key factor, contributing toward the failure prediction due to its distance from the strong scores typical of successful candidates. Overall, these top three elements—your Year 3 performance carrying the most weight—overrode positives like your income and full-time status to tip the balance. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially given your dedication to pursuing law full-time despite your background. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting metrics like LSAT and later academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework, as these have proven pivotal for students in similar situations who later succeeded. Consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to the 37+ range seen in passers, and seek programs that value your unique perspective from a lower-middle income background. Many applicants in your position have rebounded by addressing these core predictors head-on. Your path isn't closed—refining these areas could shift the odds dramatically in your favor next time.
